title-group.article-title: Persistent pain and brain fog after COVID-19 infection in European adults aged 50 and over: a population-based longitudinal study

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/042v6xz23grid.260463.50000 0001 2182 8825School of Public Health, Jiangxi Medical College, Nanchang University, Jiangxi, China

2https://ror.org/042v6xz23grid.260463.50000 0001 2182 8825Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Disease Prevention and Public Health, Jiangxi Medical College, Nanchang University, No. 461, Bayi Ave, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330006 China

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Psychological distress has been identified as a risk factor for long COVID in individuals infected with COVID-19. Little is known about the differences in long COVID symptom profiles between older adults with pre-existing depression and those without.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A population-based longitudinal prospective study was performed using the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) with participants aged 50 years and older. Depression was assessed by the EURO-D scale at the baseline. Long COVID symptoms were self-reported by participants during the 12-month follow-up. A hurdle negative binomial model was employed to assess the impact of pre-existing depression on the burden of long COVID. We compared the differences in symptoms between participants with pre-existing depression and those without with the Chi-squared test.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Participants with pre-existing depression had an approximately 16% increased risk of experiencing additional persistent symptoms after COVID-19 infection. During the 12-month follow-up following COVID-19 infection, the prevalence of headaches (32.2% vs. 25.9%; P = 0.027), body aches or joint pain (38.5% vs. 29.4%; P < 0.001), and confusion (10.6% vs. 7.6%; P < 0.01) were significantly higher among participants with pre-existing depression than among those without.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Older adults with pre-existing depression had a higher burden of long COVID following COVID-19 infection and they were more likely to suffer from persistent physical pain and confusion compared to those without a history of depression.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12879-025-11761-1.

funding-source.institution: the Senior Talent Startup Fund of Nanchang University

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long COVID, also referred to as post-COVID-19 condition (PCC), has emerged as a significant public health concern. According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), long COVID occurs in individuals with a history of probable or confirmed severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection, usually three months after the onset of COVID-19, with symptoms lasting at least two months and not attributable to an alternative diagnosis [1]. Approximately 10–35% of COVID-19 survivors experience persistent symptoms [2, 3]. The most commonly reported long COVID symptoms are persistent physical(such as fatigue, chest pain, difficulties with breathing, and painful muscles) and mental symptoms(such as memory loss and concentration difficulties) [2, 3]. Systematic review and meta-analyses demonstrated that some factors were associated with an increased risk of long COVID, including female sex, older age, smoking, severe COVID-19, pre-existing comorbidities, and obesity (high body mass index, defined as ≥ 30) [4, 5].

Depression is one of the most prevalent mental disorders, affecting approximately 3.8% of the global population before the pandemic [6]. Research has revealed a role for psychological mechanisms in long COVID [7, 8]. The underlying mechanism could relate to systemic low-grade inflammation and immune dysregulation [9–12]. Previous systematic review and meta-analysis suggested that anxiety and/or depression were associated with an increased risk of long COVID [4, 13]. Another prospective cohort study suggested that psychological distress, including depression, measured at the beginning of the pandemic was associated with the risk of persistent symptoms in individuals later infected with SARS-CoV-2, with a dose-response relationship [14]. Pre-existing anxiety and depression levels were linked to the overall number of symptoms [14, 15]. A recent study involving over 500 million COVID-19 patients revealed that pre-existing mental health conditions, including anxiety and depression, posed a comparable risk of developing long COVID as pre-existing physical health conditions [16].

In addition, several studies have suggested that psychological factors could play a role in influencing certain persistent symptoms in some patients [14, 17–19]. In a prospective cohort study, it was found that all COVID-19 symptoms (i.e., fatigue, muscle/joint/chest pain, and confusion/disorientation/brain fog), except for persistent cough and smell or taste problems, were more prevalent in participants with various types of distress, including depression, compared to those without such distress [14]. This study population was predominantly white and female and included a significant proportion of healthcare personnel, which limits the generalizability of the findings [14]. Older adults usually have multiple chronic conditions and face increased risks for infection and long COVID [4–21]. Among middle-aged and older adults in Europe, approximately 71.6% of individuals experienced at least one symptom of long COVID up to 12 months after COVID-19 infection [22]. A prospective cohort study in Hong Kong, which included 977 primary care patients aged 55 years or older with comorbid physical and psychosocial conditions, found that depression predicted post-COVID-19 fatigue [17]. The study focused primarily on the three most common post-COVID-19 symptoms (breathlessness, fatigue, and cognitive difficulty). Another study involving 84 patients (median age = 60 years) who were previously hospitalised for COVID-19 and followed up for 3 months found that depression following an acute COVID-19 episode may be associated with an increased risk of some persistent physical symptoms, including pain and dyspnea [18]. However, the differences in long COVID symptom profiles between individuals with pre-existing depression and those without, particularly among older adults are unclear.

The Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) cohort study provides a large, representative sample of European adults aged 50 and older [23]. It is also a vital resource for understanding the health and socioeconomic impacts of the pandemic on middle-aged and elderly populations [24]. This study aimed to determine differences in long COVID symptom profiles between individuals aged 50 years and older in Europe with pre-existing depression and those without to add to the existing literature.

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: We analysed a unique panel database, the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE), which serves as a valuable resource for studying the health and socioeconomic implications of the pandemic on individuals aged 50 years and older [23]. The survey data across countries were collected with a standardised sampling design and survey questionnaire. We used data from the regular SHARE questionnaire and the SHARE Corona questionnaire [23, 24].

A population-based longitudinal prospective study was performed using SHARE with participants aged 50 years and older. This cohort included participants from the baseline survey of Wave 8, which measured depression, as well as participants who took part in the SHARE Corona Surveys; 31,704 participants (aged ≥ 50) were included. Among them, 2,548 individuals had experienced COVID-19 infection. Of these, 485 participants had incomplete primary information. Ultimately, our study included a final sample of 2063 participants with complete data, of which 1611 had a positive test for the SARS-CoV-2 virus and 452 experienced symptoms that they attributed to the COVID-19 illness but were not tested. Further details can be found in Supplementary Figure S1. Ethics approval was handled by SHARE (Survey of Health, Aging, and retirement in Europe) [25]. Informed consent to participate was also obtained by SHARE, with all participants providing their consent to be included in the study [25].

sec.title: Measurement of depression
sec.p: In this study, participants with depression were defined as having a depression symptom score above established cut-off points, which were assessed with the EURO-D mental health scale. It determines the presence of negative affect and somatic symptoms on a scale from 0 (the lowest level of depression) to 12 (the highest), which covers 12 items (depression, pessimism, wishing death, guilt, sleep, interest, irritability, appetite, fatigue, concentration, enjoyment, and tearfulness). Clinically significant depression is defined as a EURO-D score of 4 or higher on the basis of validation work conducted among older adults [26].

We employed the recommended thresholds (EURO-D score ≥ 4) to identify participants with depression. The measurement of the EURO-D scores was conducted before the outbreak of the pandemic (October 2019-March 2020). Although the EURO-D score does not provide a definitive diagnosis of depression such as in the DSM-IV or ICD-10, this cut-off score shows good predictive performance [27].

sec.title: Long COVID
sec.p: The participants were asked whether they had experienced any long-term or lingering effects that they attributed to their COVID-19 illness. They were presented with a list of nine symptoms, including fatigue, cough, congestion, shortness of breath, loss of taste or smell, headache, body aches/joint pain, chest or abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, confusion, or any other symptoms. In a telephone interview, participants were required to check all that apply and read out, and then choose all applicable responses.

To quantify the extent of symptoms, a count variable ranging from 0 (indicating no symptoms) to 9 (representing the maximum number of symptoms) was created. In the present study, participants were considered to have long COVID, if they reported one or more of the long-term or lingering symptoms during the 12-month follow-up after COVID-19 infection (from June/August 2020 to June/August 2021). This assumption was supported by several studies [21, 28–32].

sec.title: Covariates
sec.p: Covariates in this study included sociodemographic indicators (gender, age, level of education, living arrangement, work situation, and financial status), body mass index (BMI, mass in kg divided by square of height in meters), smoking history, seven chronic conditions (history of heart attack, high blood pressure or hypertension, stroke or cerebral vascular disease, diabetes or high blood sugar, chronic lung disease, cancer, and chronic kidney disease), and number of chronic conditions. All covariates were collected during Wave 8 of the SHARE (fielded in October 2019-March 2020) or SHARE Corona Survey 1 (June-August 2020).

Gender was measured as a dichotomous variable indicating male or female sex. Age was classified into two groups (50–69 and ≥ 70 years), consistent with previous studies [21, 22, 33]. Education level was categorised into three groups according to the International Standard Classification of Education 1997: low education (ISCED-97 levels 0–2), medium education (ISCED-97 levels 3–4), and high education (ISCED-97 levels 5–6). Living arrangements are measured by whether an individual lives with a partner or spouse.

Work situations encompassed two groups before the pandemic (not employed vs. employed). Financial status was assessed as a binary variable and included two categories: difficulty making ends meet and easily making ends meet during the outbreak of the pandemic (October 2019 - March 2020).
BMI was categorised into two groups (< 30 and ≥ 30). Smoking history refers to participants who had a record of smoking cigarettes, cigars, cigarillos, or a pipe daily for at least one year.

Chronic conditions were diagnosed by medical doctors or specialists and reported by self or proxy- participants on follow-up questionnaires, including the following seventeen chronic conditions: heart attack, high blood pressure or hypertension, high blood cholesterol, stroke or cerebral vascular disease, diabetes or high blood sugar, chronic lung disease, cancer or malignant tumour, stomach or duodenal ulcer, Parkinson’s disease, cataracts, hip fracture, other fractures, Alzheimer’s disease or any other serious memory impairment, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis or other rheumatism, chronic kidney disease, and other conditions. Information on chronic conditions was collected in Wave 8 and the Corona Survey 1, depending on when the participants first reported these chronic conditions. In this study, the average number of chronic conditions was 1.95. A total of 79.8% of the participants had at least one of these chronic conditions. A total of 55.02% of the participants had ≥ 2 these chronic conditions.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: Descriptive data for baseline characteristics of participants are presented as mean (standard deviation, SD) values or frequency (percentage), as appropriate. Chi-squared test was used to compare the baseline characteristics.

A hurdle negative binomial model was employed to assess the impact of pre-existing depression on the risk of long COVID and on the number of symptoms. Among all participants, the average number of symptoms is 2.25, with a variance of 4.32, which implies that the count data are excessively discrete (variance greater than the mean), subject to the assumption of a negative binomial distribution. Country variance was used as a random effect in the model to capture potential differences.

The model is structured in two parts: the zero part estimates the probability of an individual reporting zero symptoms. In contrast, the count part is the count of occurrences given that the event has occurred [34, 35]. In our study, the event referred to long COVID symptoms. Adjusted rate ratios (aORs) with 95% CIs for the risk of long COVID and adjusted rate ratios (aRRs) with 95% CIs for the level of symptoms were calculated in the binary model and the count model, respectively.

Furthermore, we compared the frequencies and proportions of 9 long COVID symptoms between participants with pre-existing depression and those without pre-existing depression with Chi-squared test. The significance level was a priori at P < 0.05, with P values from all tests being corrected using the Holm-Bonferroni correction [36].

All statistical tests were conducted with R (version 4.3.2). A multilevel (random intercept) hurdle negative binomial model was performed using the “mixed_model” function in the GLMMadaptive package in R [37]. All P values were from 2-sided tests and results were deemed statistically significant at P < 0.05.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of the 2063 participants who contracted COVID-19 (mean age, 68.0(8.5) years), 1259(61%) were women, and 603 (29.2%) had a pre-existing depression (Table 1). The proportion of females was greater among participants with pre-existing depression than among participants without pre-existing depression (72.6% vs. 56.2%; P < 0.0001). Participants with pre-existing depression were older than those without (mean, 69.1 (SD = 9.3) vs. 67.5 (SD = 8.1) years; P < 0.0001) and were more likely to have ≥ 2 chronic conditions (67.2% vs. 50%, P < 0.0001). In addition, a greater proportion of participants with pre-existing depression than those without pre-existing depression included those who did not live with a partner or spouse, had lower levels of education, had more difficult family economic situations, and were unemployed status (Table 1).

caption.p: Characteristics of participants according to two groups: without depression and with depression before the pandemic

tr.td: Living with a partner or spouse, n(%)

sec.p: Pre-existing depression did not have a statistically significant effect on the risk of long COVID (at least one long COVID symptom) (adjusted OR = 0.99; 95% CI = 0.78–1.26), after adjusting for sociodemographic characteristics (gender, age, level of education, living arrangement), financial status, work situation, BMI, smoking status, and the number of chronic conditions (Table 2).

caption.p: Zero symptoms and average of symptoms for participants with depression

table-wrap-foot.p: * Significant at P < 0.05, ** <0.01, ***<0.001, and ****<0.0001; CI: Confidence interval
Model 2: adjusted sociodemographic (age, gender, living arrangement, and education);
Model 3: additional controlled financial distress and employment status;
Model 4: further adjusted BMI and smoking;
Model 5: further adjusted number of chronic conditions

sec.p: Among individuals reporting long COVID symptoms, individuals with pre-existing depression reported more persisting symptoms compared to those without pre-existing (3.33 vs. 2.98). Unadjusted analysis showed that pre-existing depression was significantly related to a higher number of symptoms associated with long COVID (RR = 1.18; 95% CI = 1.09–1.27) (Table 2). After adjusting for adjusted sociodemographic factors (age, sex, living arrangement, education), pre-existing depression was also associated with a greater number of symptoms (aRR = 1.18; 95% CI = (1.09, 1.28). When additional controlled financial distress and employment status, the aRR value decreased slightly (aRR = 1.17; 95% CI = 1.07–1.27). Furthermore, pre-existing depression remained significantly related to a greater number of symptoms with a slightly lower aRR value when further adjusted for the number of chronic conditions (aRR = 1.16; 95% CI = 1.07–1.26) (Table 2). More detailed information is provided in Supplementary Tables S1-S3.

The most frequent symptoms were followed by fatigue (50.4%), cough/congestion/shortness of breath (36.5%), and body aches/joint pain (32.0%). A greater number of individuals with pre-existing depression reported headache (32.2% vs. 25.9%; P value = 0.027), body aches/joint pain (38.5% vs. 29.4%; P value < 0.001), and confusion(10.6% vs. 7.6%;P value < 0.01) than those without pre-existing depression (Fig. 1). P values from all tests were corrected via the Holm-Bonferroni correction.

caption.p: Differences of long COVID symptoms between adults aged 50 or above with and without pre-existing depression following COVID-19 All P values were adjusted by using the Holm-Bonferroni correction * Significant at P < 0.05, ** <0.01, and ***<0.001

sec.title: Discussions
sec.p: This is the first study to examine differences in the risks and symptom profiles of long COVID between individuals with pre-existing depression and those without pre-existing depression using SHARE. We found that among individuals reporting long COVID symptoms, individuals with pre-existing depression had an approximately 16% increased risk of experiencing additional symptoms. In this study, we compared nine long-term or persistent effects after contracting COVID-19 between the depression group and the non-depression group. A greater number of individuals with pre-existing depression reported headache, body aches/joint pain, and confusion than those without pre-existing depression. These results strengthen the evidence available regarding the association of pre-existing depression with long COVID symptoms [18].

In line with previous studies, pre-existing depression was associated with the number of total symptoms [14, 15]. A previous prospective study also showed that individuals with probable depression reported a greater number of symptoms of long COVID (mean (SD) symptoms = 3.4 (2.1) vs. no depression, mean (SD) symptoms = 2.5(1.7)) [14]. Another study also revealed that levels of anxiety and depression were both associated with the burden of long COVID [15].

Additionally, the results of our study revealed that some persistent symptoms, including headache, body aches/joint pain, and confusion, were more prevalent in participants with pre-existing depression compared to those without. Our results differ somewhat from the results of Zhang et al. [17] who suggested in primary care patients aged 55 years or above with comorbid physical and psychosocial conditions, depression was a predictor of post-COVID-19 fatigue. We found no evidence suggesting that older people with pre-existing depression reported more persistent fatigue than those without pre-existing depression. We found that older people with pre-existing depression reported more headache, and body aches/joint pain. This aligns with previous research suggesting that depression may increase the risk of persistent physical symptoms, including pain [18]. Depression is associated with a complex interplay between chronic pain and inflammatory processes [38], which may explain the observation of increased symptoms of headaches or body aches/joint pain in individuals with depression.

Individuals suffering from long-COVID can present with “brain fog”, which is characterised by a range of cognitive impairments, such as a lack of intellectual clarity, mental fatigue, confusion, memory loss, and difficulty concentrating [39, 40]. Our data indicated that a greater proportion of individuals with pre-existing depression reported experiencing long-term confusion during the 12-month follow-up in older adults who contracted COVID-19. Similarly, a study demonstrated that depression was the strongest predictor of persistent brain fog 1 year after COVID-19 disease [41]. In a nationwide population cohort study on the natural history of long COVID, it was found that the prevalence of confusion six months after symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection was significantly higher among individuals with a history of depression or anxiety than among those without such a history [42]. Additionally, individuals with pre-existing depression or anxiety demonstrated less improvement in confusion over time [42]. As noted in a retrospective cohort study, pre-existing mental health diagnoses were independently linked to subjective neurocognitive impairment at 8 months after infection [43]. This study also supports our findings.

Evidence indicates that self-reported brain fog/confusion can reflect global objective cognitive performance in long COVID patients, including older adults [32, 39, 44, 45]. Some key features in long COVID-associated brain fog have been identified in previous mechanism studies, including persistent localised blood-brain barrier disruption, gene alterations, and elevated brain injury markers - all of which appear independent of age [45–47]. Brain fog represents a severe manifestation of long COVID, further research is needed to investigate the impact of cognitive impairment after contracting COVID-19 in the vulnerable older adults.

This study has several limitations. First, this study has inherent limitations due to its observational design, which means that it can only establish associations but not causation. Although efforts were made to control for confounding factors, there may still be residual confounding that could influence the observed relationships between variables. Second, this study relied on self-reported data, which introduces the possibility of recall bias and misclassification. Additionally, the questionnaire did not provide explanatory information for the term “persistent confusion”, potentially leading to further misclassification due to participants’ varying interpretations of the term. Third, due to participants were not asked to provide temporal details of persistent symptoms, we were unable to apply a standard definition of long COVID (e.g. WHO or NICE definitions). The prevalence of long COVID may be overestimated since the patient-reported symptoms could also occur within the 12 weeks after infection [1, 48]. Fourth, the single indicator of persisting confusion may fail to capture the complexity of cognitive impairment due to COVID-19. Therefore, the findings should be interpreted with caution. Finally, depression was measured using a validated scale among older adults rather than clinical diagnoses, while this scale has been widely used and validated in previous research.

Nevertheless, this study also has some potential implications. It highlights the importance to enhance identification and ongoing monitoring of high-risk populations. When managing long COVID, clinicians may need to assess if older adults with a history of depression have comorbid cognitive impairments after contracting COVID-19. Future pandemic responses should incorporate targeted surveillance systems to track health outcomes in high-risk groups.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This population-based study found that older adults with pre-existing depression experienced a greater burden of long COVID after COVID-19 infection, and they were more likely to experience persistent physical pain and confusion than those without a history of depression. Policymakers and healthcare providers need to pay greater attention to this vulnerable group and provide appropriate support and resources.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: WG acquired funding for this study. WG supervised the study. XH conducted the literature search and performed data collection, management, and analysis. XH drafted the manuscript. WG directed the study and revised the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by the Senior Talent Startup Fund of Nanchang University.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Data from the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) (https://share-eric.eu/) are available to all researchers upon making an application.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The SHARE Project respects the Declaration of Helsinki in terms of anonymity of the participants; The internal review board at the University of Mannheim and the Ethics Council of the Max Planck Society and the respective local ethics committees or institutional review boards in each country participating in SHARE have approved this study. More information about the ethical approval is available at the following link: (https://share-eric.eu/fileadmin/user_upload/Ethics_Documentation/SHARE_ethics_approvals.pdf). Ethics approval was handled by SHARE (Survey of Health, Aging, and retirement in Europe). See: A., Jürges, Hendrik. The Survey of Health, Aging, and Retirement in Europe-Methodology. MEA, 2005. Informed consent to participate also was obtained by SHARE, all participants gave their informed consent to be included in the study.

notes.title: Consent for publication
notes.p: Consent was obtained by SHARE (Survey of Health, Aging, and retirement in Europe). See: Börsch-Supan, A., Jürges, Hendrik. The Survey of Health, Aging, and Retirement in Europe-Methodology. MEA, 2005.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

element-citation.article-title: Determinants of post-COVID-19 symptoms among adults aged 55 or above with chronic conditions in primary care: data from a prospective cohort in Hong Kong

citation-alternatives.mixed-citation: Zhang DX, Chung VCH, Chan DCC, Xu ZJ, Zhou WJ, Tam KW, et al.
